coredata.dc:title: Experimental and numerical studies on lithium-ion battery heat generation behaviors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Chongtian
Wu, Linxu
Qiu, Chenghui
Yang, Jiaming
Yuan, Xiaolu
Cai, Yufei
Shi, Hong
coredata.dc:description:
Current predictions of battery HGR (heat generation rate) mainly rely on Bernardi’s empirical equations, which suffer from limitations of adaptability for thermal use. A novel scheme based on experiments and BPNN (BP (back propagation) neural network is a multilayer feedforward neural network trained according to the error back propagation algorithm) is proposed in this paper. In the experiments, the thermal generation behavior of 18650 batteries under 108 different operating conditions was investigated, and the
T
a
(ambient temperature) as well as
I
D
(discharge current) were extracted as inputs to the BPNN model. The experimental results show that the heat generation performance of the battery is strongly influenced by
T
a
and
I
D
. The higher
T
a
and
I
D
, the more obvious this effect is. The maximum error of the battery HGR obtained by using Bernardi’s empirical equations can reach 45.2 %. The predicted battery HGR using BPNN has good accuracy with error control within 5%. Accordingly, this paper provides the technical basis for proposing a promising thermal management solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Heat generation rate prediction
Battery thermal management
Bernardi equation
BPNN

Geometry model and main governing equations
Prediction of HGR based on BPNN

I

at 25 °C.

a
at 4 A discharge.

f
at different

s.

row.entry:
T
f
of the experiment (
° C
)

head.ce:title: Experimental and numerical studies on lithium-ion battery heat generation behaviors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Energy & Power Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, 2 Mengxi, Jingkou, Zhenjiang 212003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Energy & Power Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Energy & Power Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, 2 Mengxi, Jingkou, Zhenjiang 212003, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Current predictions of battery HGR (heat generation rate) mainly rely on Bernardi’s empirical equations, which suffer from limitations of adaptability for thermal use. A novel scheme based on experiments and BPNN (BP (back propagation) neural network is a multilayer feedforward neural network trained according to the error back propagation algorithm) is proposed in this paper. In the experiments, the thermal generation behavior of 18650 batteries under 108 different operating conditions was investigated, and the
T
a
(ambient temperature) as well as
I
D
(discharge current) were extracted as inputs to the BPNN model. The experimental results show that the heat generation performance of the battery is strongly influenced by
T
a
and
I
D
. The higher
T
a
and
I
D
, the more obvious this effect is. The maximum error of the battery HGR obtained by using Bernardi’s empirical equations can reach 45.2 %. The predicted battery HGR using BPNN has good accuracy with error control within 5%. Accordingly, this paper provides the technical basis for proposing a promising thermal management solution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request

ce:section.ce:para:
New energy electric vehicles are gradually developing due to their advantages such as low energy consumption and less pollution (Xu, 2021; Al-Zareer, 2020; Shelkea, 2022; Zhang et al., 2022). As the most important component of new energy electric vehicles, lithium batteries play a key role in the conversion of chemical and electrical energy. However, lithium batteries have always suffered from thermal runaway, reduced energy utilization, and weakened power output under extreme environmental conditions (Wu et al., 2021). Current cooling methods for battery systems include air cooling, liquid cooling (Sirikasemsuk et al., 2021; Wiriyasart, 2020; Jang et al., 2022) and phase change material cooling, but the main cause of thermal runaway in battery packs is the unreasonable control of individual battery heat sources so it is especially important to study the heat generation behavior of individual battery. Mevawalla et al. (2022) simulate the internal resistance and surface temperature of the battery by modeling different dimensions of the battery under different operating conditions, using actual measurable parameters. Panchal et al. (2022) proposed a thermal execution modeling method for microchannel coolant plates for electric vehicle battery cooling. Combining experiments and simulations, they found that the temperature contours of the cooling plate increased as the C-rate (C-rate (C) is the measurement of the charge and discharge current with respect to its nominal capacity) and ambient temperature increased. Drake (2015) innovatively proposed a novel method for measuring the internal temperature of batteries by investigating HGR (heat generation rate) and HGR of batteries during high current discharge. He (2022) found that the main heat generation source of the battery is at the negative electrode by building a heat generation model of the battery in different dimensions and when the convective heat transfer coefficient of the battery surface was smaller, HGR of the battery was higher. Zhang (2020) proposed a method for estimating the heat generation of lithium batteries based on dual-temperature measurement and two-state thermal model, which can accurately estimate total heat generation of lithium batteries. Xie et al. (2018) proposed a new model of the battery lumped parameter model based on the air-cooling system and fitted the empirical equations of the battery HGR by experiments and simulations. They found that the effect of
T
a
(ambient temperature) on the battery heating rate varies when the DOD (depth of discharge) is in different ranges. Yal (2022) proposed a convolutional neural network-artificial bee colony algorithm to predict HGR and voltage of lithium batteries at different ambient temperatures and discharge currents. Amini et al. (2021) built an entropy term estimation procedure and combined it with a thermal-vehicle dynamics model to predict the thermal behavior of the battery under different operating conditions. Wang et al. (2022) constructed a resistance-based thermal model of the battery and analyzed how air velocity and temperature affect the maximum battery temperature. Xie et al. (2022) proposed a distributed spatio-temporal online correction algorithm for state of charge (SOC)-3D state of temperature (SOT) co-estimation by combining SOC estimation with a distributed temperature online recovery algorithm. Jindal et al. (2022) measured the actual HGR of NMC (LiNi 0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2) and LFP (LiFePO4) lithium batteries by continuous discharge and found that although Bernardi’s heat generation equation provides a good estimation of the heat generation of the battery, it still has a large error in many practical operating conditions. Farag (2017) proposed a numerical simulation method with coupled electrochemical–thermal–heat​ generation model, which can accurately obtain the end voltage and central temperature of lithium batteries. Tourani (2014) found that the performance of lithium batteries decreased sharply when
T
a
< 7 °C, while the performance of lithium batteries barely improved when
T
a
> 27 °C.
T
f
(final temperature) of the battery was a function of
T
a
. These above findings also demonstrate the existence of strict environmental temperature have limits on the use of HGR calculation method for batteries.

From the above research work, although researchers have carried out a lot of research on HGR under different operating conditions, they have not given a universal method for calculating HGR of batteries. In fact, the classical Bernardi’s heat generation equation is mostly used in practical applications. However, battery materials, packaging and other technologies have improved significantly. The adaptability of the classical Bernardi’s equation has arisen in doubt. Therefore, exploring a highly accurate method for calculating HGR of the battery has become a primary issue in battery thermal runaway research. HGR of a battery is significantly related to the environmental parameters and
I
C
(charge current) and
I
D
(discharge current). It is a typical multi-parameter problem and traditional empirical fitting methods are not suitable.

In this paper, based on the analysis of existing methods for calculating HGR of batteries, experiments were carried out on the heat generation law of 18650 lithium batteries. Effects of environmental parameters and
I
D
on the heat generation of the battery were investigated. Besides, the error between the simulated HGR of battery and HGR calculated by Bernardi’s heat generation equation was predicted based on BPNN (BP (back propagation) neural network is a multilayer feedforward neural network trained according to the error back propagation algorithm) method. The Bernardi-based model for calculating HGR of batteries has been found to exhibit a large error when compared to experimental results. Moreover, it has become apparent that the traditional empirical formula is not reliable for obtaining accurate battery HGR values. Unfortunately, few researchers have studied the actual heat generation characteristics of batteries, and most still rely on empirical formulas for battery thermal runaway research. This can lead to significant deviations between the obtained results and the actual behavior of the battery, which poses a significant safety risk.

Good familiarity with battery dissipation mechanisms is essential for understanding the thermal behaviors of lithium-ion batteries. Battery structure generally consists of five main parts: the positive electrode (cathode), the separator, the shell, the electrolyte, and the negative electrode (anode). The anode is usually made up of a mixture of carbon (e.g. Li
x
C6), the cathode is typically made of metal oxides (ex. LiCoO2 or LiMn 2O4) (Huang et al., 2017). Fig. 1 shows the specific heat generation mechanisms of a battery. Lithium batteries are filled with electrolyte inside and have high conductivity for lithium ions. The lithium ions transferred between the cathode and anode of the battery occur a series of chemical reactions inside the battery to generate heat.

According to the heat generation mechanism, the heat generation in the battery Q voltage-based (Verma and Rakshit, 2022) is defined by Eq. (1):
(1)
Q
b a t
=
I
b a t
U
W V
−
U
O C V
+
T
b a t
∂
U
O C V
∂
T
b c t
where I, U, T and
∂
U
O C V
/ ∂ T
represents the current, the voltage, temperature and the entropy coefficient, respectively. Subscript bat, WV and OCV are the battery, the working voltage and the open circuit voltage, respectively.

Eq. (1) divides the heat generation of the battery into two parts, the first term is the irreversible heat (ohmic heat) and the other is Δ S (entropy change) caused by the electrochemical reaction (Xu, 2022).

The irreversible heat
Q
i r
can be defined as Eq. (2):
(2)
Q
i r
=
I
b a t
2
R
O
+
R
P
where R is the resistance. Subscript O and P are the ohmic and the polarization, respectively. Substituting the
I
U
W V
−
U
O C V
in Eq. (1) with Eq. (2) gives the resistance-based heat generation model which is:
(3)
Q =
I
b a t
2
R
O
+
R
P
+
I
b a t
T
b a t
∂
U
O C V
∂ T

Eq. (3) shows that the heat generation of the battery is closely related to
R
i
,
T
a
and
I
D
of the battery. Furthermore, in the Ref of Doh (2019) it is known that the differential time can be directly correlated with differential SOC by using Eq. (5), so the battery HGR can be directly correlated with the C-rate by Eq. (7):
(4)
Q = ∫
I
b a t
2
R
i
−
1
K
I
b a t
2
T
b a t
Δ S d t
(5)
S O C =
q
0
−
I
t
q
0
− 1
(6)
d t = −
q
0
I
− 1
d S O C
(7)
I = C − r a t e ⋅
q
0
where K,
q
0
and C-rate represent Faraday’s constant, battery capacity and the measurement of
I
C
and
I
D
with respect to its nominal capacity. Subscript t is the time, respectively.

Combining the above equations, it is clear that the battery HGR is also related to SOC, S, C-rate and other factors. However, the more widely used equation for battery heat rate calculations remains the empirical correlation proposed by Bernardi in 1980. Certainly, many scholars have also studied Bernardi’s empirical equation with certain extensions. Yet, there are still many bottlenecks in the actual engineering application process. For the equation, by varying the current and
T
a
, it is found that the current has a much greater effect on HGR than
T
a
, which appears to be less important. However, in practical situations, we find that the effect of
T
a
on the heating of the battery cannot be ignored. In addition, there is a large error between the calculated value of HGR and the experimental value when the battery heat dissipation is directly simulated by the volumetric HGR. Therefore, accurate prediction of HGR of the battery is the basis of current battery heat dissipation.

The experimental apparatus is shown in Fig. 2. The experiment mainly consists of a computer, discharging device (Model: LANHE), a K-type thermocouple, multi-channel temperature recorder (Model: CTR-380), an anemometer (Model: testo 410-1), a thermostat (Model: MCGS) and an 18650 battery. Table 1 presents the battery specifications.

Using a spot welder, the cathode and anode of the battery are firmly welded to the nickel sheet and the battery is connected to the discharge device, after which thermocouples were affixed near the cathode and anode of the battery. To ensure thermocouple stability, thermocouples were arranged vertically on the cathode and anode of the battery and placed in the thermostat, the different
T
a
can be adjusted through the thermostat control panel. After the temperature had stabilized, the anemometer was used to measure the wind velocity flowing over the battery surface to obtain the accurate convective heat transfer coefficient of the battery surface, providing accurate boundary conditions for the next section of simulation, so that the simulation can be more closely matched with the experiment to obtain more accurate HGR. The battery was discharged by adjusting the monitoring software in the computer then used the discharge device to achieve a stable constant current discharge. In the process of battery heat generation, CTR-380 multi-channel temperature recorder and K-type thermocouple converted the heat signal from the battery surface into an electrical signal to the temperature patrol meter, so as to obtain the temperature of the battery surface. To reduce the error of the experiment, the surface temperature of the battery was recorded every 100 s and the average value was taken to obtain
T
f
of the battery.

The experimental procedure consists of three main steps, as shown in Fig. 3. In the first step,
T
f
of the battery under different operating conditions was obtained by adjusting the temperature of the thermostat and
I
C
and
I
D
of the battery. In the second step, the battery HGR was calculated for each operating condition based on Bernardi’s empirical equation. The third step was to establish a database of HGR at different
T
a
and battery
I
D
.

It is worth noting that each 18650-lithium battery should be fully charged before each experiment. Then, after temperature was set in the thermostat, the experiment should be kept for a certain period to ensure a stable thermal equilibrium between the battery and the interior environment of thermostat. Finally, the discharge experiment was then performed with a constant pulse current to obtain
T
f
.

After the end of discharge, the above experimental process was repeated for the next working condition and recorded as Table 2 (only a part of it was listed due to the problem of space). A total of 108 sets of experimental data under different working conditions can be obtained by orthogonalization
T
a
(12 groups) and
I
D
(9 groups).

The uncertainties and accuracies of the experimental instruments are shown in Table 3, the maximum uncertainty of h obtained from the calculation process is
± 5
%, calculated from the following equation:
(8)
Uncertainty of
A
=
∂ A
∂ V
Δ V
2
+
∂ A
∂
T
s
Δ
T
s
2
+
∂ A
∂
T
B
Δ
T
B
2
+
∂ h
∂
T
F
Δ
T
F
2
where A, V, B and F represent Uncertainty velocity, send, back and fluid, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery HGR obtained by combining Bernardi’s equation and experiment
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to the large amount of data, the thermocouple temperature plot at an ambient temperature of 29–37 °C at 6 A is given in this paper. From Fig. 4, it can be seen that the higher the ambient temperature the battery is exposed to at the same
I
D
, the lower the growth rate of
T
f
under the condition of increasing the same ambient temperature.

Based on
T
f
of the battery which was measured under different operating conditions, HGR of the battery is calculated using Bernardi’s equation. These results of battery HGR are given in Fig. 5.

As shown in Fig. 5, the calculated HGR of battery increases with the increase of
I
D
. At the same temperature, the maximum increase in HGR at different
I
D
is 66.5%. However, there is almost little difference in the increase rate of HGR at the same
I
D
and at different
T
a
. The increase in HGR relative to 5 A discharge is 27.5% and 28.0% when the battery is discharged at 17 °C and 27 °C for 6 A, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometry model and main governing equations
ce:section.ce:para: The battery heat generation module of the numerical study used in the present study shown in Fig. 6. In this section, the battery model was meshed using ICEM and the grid independence verification determined that 758535 grid cells were used for the numerical simulation.
The problem was analyzed on the basis of the assumptions of neglecting viscous dissipation and radiation, incompressible flow of fluids, and constant properties.

The following are the airflow equations, which include the continuity and momentum equations:
(9)
∇ ⋅
ρ
f
⋅ v
= 0
(10)
∇ ⋅
ρ
f
⋅ v v
= − ∇ P + ∇ ⋅
μ
f
⋅ ∇ v
where ρ , v, P and μ represents density, dynamic viscosity, pressure, kinematic viscosity, respectively.

In the simulation, the following energy equation was solved to obtain the temperature distribution:
(11)
ρ
C
p
∂ T
∂ t
=
∂
∂ x
λ
x
∂ T
∂ x
+
∂
∂ y
λ
y
∂ T
∂ y
+
∂
∂ z
λ
z
∂ T
∂ z
+ Q
where
C
P
, λ represents heat capacity, thermal conductivity, respectively.

Fig. 6 shows that in the thermostat, the lithium battery discharges heat and its outer surface exchanges heat with the surrounding air through convection. Due to the anisotropy of battery, this paper chose
λ
x
=
λ
y
= 1.17 and
λ
z
= 37.91 as λ of battery, respectively (Yang et al., 2021). The convection heat transfer equation is given by Eq. (12):
(12)
Q =
h
a , s u r
T
s u r
−
T
a
where T is the temperature, °C, h is the convection heat transfer coefficient, W/m2 °C. Subscript a and sur represents ambient and surface, respectively.

Since the convective heat transfer coefficient is different at different
T
a
, calculated by the equation (Malekshah, 2018):
(13)
h
a , s u r
=
N u ×
λ
a
H
where H is the height, m, Nu is the Nusselt number.
Based on the computational domain. The k-e turbulence model was chosen to study the transient heat generation behavior of the battery. The boundary conditions for the simulation are as follows:
- Constant heat flux
-Walls: Corresponding h and no slip condition for all directions
-Anode and cathode: Ni material
-Middle part of the battery: Al material

The time step is set to 1 s, and the number of time steps corresponds to the discharge time, The numerical computation is ended if the residual summed over all the computational nodes satisfies the criterion of 10−5.

To verify the accuracy of numerical simulation, the experimental and simulation contents of Zhang et al. (2022) were simulated in this paper. The experiments of. Zhang et al. (2022) adopt a variety of heat generation methods, and in this paper, the simulation is based on the working condition of
T
a
= 25 °C and HGR of 8 C. The current simulation also monitors the average battery surface temperature with the same initial conditions as in Zhang et al. (2022) as follows: h
= 38 W/m2 °C,
T
b a t
= T
a
= 25 °C.

As shown in Fig. 7, the simulation results obtained in this paper basically match the experimental and simulation results of Zhang et al. (2022) with the maximum error within 4%, which proves the accuracy of the simulation.

The simulation ensured that the average battery temperature of the simulation was consistent with the average temperature of the experiment by obtaining a battery HGR using the trial-and-error method. The measured thermal–physical parameters of the Li-ion battery were input into the Fluent simulation software and the temperature distribution of the battery surface was obtained. Figs. 8 and 9 show the temperature distribution of battery discharge at different
I
D
at 25 °C and at different
T
a
at 4 A discharge, respectively.

As it can be seen from Fig. 8.
T
f
increases rapidly when
I
D
is greater than 4 A. When
I
D
= 6 A,
T
f
is about 43 °C, while when
I
D
= 2 A,
T
f
is only 27 °C. Further, when the battery is discharged at 6 A, its
T
f
rises about 17.8% with respect to
I
D
= 5 A, whereas
I
D
= 3 A, its
T
f
rises only 5.9% relative to
I
D
= 2 A.

As it can be seen in Fig. 9, The rate of temperature changing with increasing
T
a
is higher for the same
I
D
at less than 25 °C. Specifically, the rates are 10.4% and 20.5% at
T
a
of 21 °C and 25 °C, respectively. However, at
T
a
of 29 °C and 33 °C, these rates are only 7.7% and 6.9%, respectively.
The simulated HGR results are shown in Fig. 10.

Fig. 10 shows that the simulated HGR also increases with the increase of
I
D
. However, the simulated and experimental values are significantly different. Furthermore, when the battery is discharged at high
T
a
and high
I
D
, the increase of HGR will be suppressed and gradually stabilized. In particular, at
T
a
= 37 °C and
I
D
= 6 A, the HGR increases by only 5.5% relative to
T
a
= 37 °C and
I
D
= 5.5 A. However, at the same discharge conditions and
T
a
= 15 °C, HGR increased by 27.2%.
Therefore, the difference between the simulated HGR and calculated HGR was studied by comparing HGR calculated from the experiment and simulation, as shown in Fig. 11.

From Fig. 11, when the battery is discharged at different
T
a
, the simulated HGR is significantly different from HGR obtained based on the empirical equation, and the maximum difference can reach 43636 W/m3, with an error of 45.2%, which indicates that there are obvious defects in the simulation prediction using the empirical equation for HGR.

Fig. 12 shows HGR at different
I
D
at 17 °C, 27 °C and 37 °C.

As can be seen from Fig. 12, When
I
D
is less than 3.5 A, HGR of the battery with
T
a
of 37 °C is greater than that of 27 °C, but when
I
D
is greater than 3.5 A, HGR of the battery with
T
a
of 27 °C significantly exceeds that of 37 °C. It is proved that the higher the
T
a
is, the greater the suppression of the increase in HGR. When the
I
D
is 6 A, the difference between HGR of the
T
a
of 17 °C and 37 °C reaches 44000. While, the difference of HGR between
T
a
of 17 °C and 27 °C is 7000. However, it does not guide that the battery is suitable for charging and discharging at higher
T
a
.

Fig. 13 compares
T
f
of battery discharge at
T
a
of 37 °C, 27 °C and 17 °C.

As can be seen from Fig. 13, when 6 A discharge is performed on both batteries, the difference between
T
f
of the battery at a
T
a
of 37 °C and the
T
f
of the battery at a
T
a
of 17 °C reaches 11 °C. Even
T
f
of the battery discharged at a
T
a
of 37 °C will reach 47.5 °C. Obviously, such temperature is not the appropriate temperature for battery operation, far exceeding the appropriate operating temperature range of 20 °C ∼ 40 °C for batteries. Therefore, while high current discharge of the battery at 37 °C may not result in a high HGR, it may cause irreversible damage to the Li-ion battery. It is critical to find a balance between HGR and
T
f
of the battery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction of HGR based on BPNN

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the defects of the empirical equation for battery HGR, this paper proposes to employ BPNN to predict the error, so as to obtain a correction method of HGR based on Bernardi’s empirical equation. The establishment of BPNN includes four steps, establishing the neural network structure, setting the network training parameters, dividing and preprocessing the data, and evaluating BPNN. The specific correction method is shown in Fig. 14.

The input layer had two nodes, which were
T
a
and
I
D
. The output layer had one node, which was different between simulated HGR and calculated HGR. The number of nodes in the hidden layer can be calculated by the following equation (Geng and Wang, 2020):
(14)
S =
m + n
+ k
where S is the number of nodes in the hidden layer, m and n are the number of nodes in the input and output layers, respectively, and,
k ∈
( 1 , 10 )
. According to the empirical equation, the number of nodes in the hidden layer is determined to be 12, and finally a 2-12-1 neural network structure is obtained.
In fact, to better understand the procedure of BPNN for making predictions, we built a flowchart, as shown in Fig. 15.

Since the parameters of network training were closely related to the accuracy and convergence speed of network prediction, the network training parameters should be appropriately set. After repeated training, “Tansing” was selected as the input layer transfer function and “Purelin” was selected as the output layer transfer function. In addition, “Trainlm” was chosen as the training function with a learning rate of 0.01. Further, the target of training error was set to 10−3 and the number of steps was set to 1000.

In this paper, the neural network was constructed by integrating a total of 108 sets of samples from both experimental data and simulation data. Each set of samples is composed of
I
D
,
T
a
and difference of HGR, where the difference of HGR is the difference between the real battery HGR obtained from the simulation and the theoretical HGR calculated by the empirical formula. And 80% of them were randomly selected for network training, and the remaining 20% data points were used as test data to evaluate the prediction ability of the BPNN model. To improve the prediction accuracy and convergence speed, the input data were normalized and the output data were denormalized so that the input values were designed between −1 and 1. The input and output data were normalized according to Eqs. (15) and (16) as follows (Yu, 2014):
(15)
D
n
=
D
0
−
D
min
D
max
−
D
min
(16)
D
0
=
D
min
+
D
n
D
max
−
D
min
where D is the data. Subscript 0, max, min and n are the initial value, the maximum, the minimum and the normalized data, respectively.

The accuracy of the BPNN model was a systematically evaluated with R2 and MARE (Yuan et al., 2022). The R2
and MARE (mean absolute relative error) represent the coefficient of determination of the model and the predictability of the model, respectively.
Figs. 16–19 presents the comparison of predicted and expected values, the best validation in terms of mean square error, the training state plot, and the error histogram, respectively.

Fig. 16 shows that the predicted value is close to the expected value, these errors do not exceed. Further, R2 and MARE are 0.9600 and 0.53%, respectively, indicating that the BPNN model correlated the battery HGR. From Fig. 17, it can be seen that the MSE (mean square error) of BPNN is 0.035831 at epoch 2, and the test and validation curves have very small deviations, which is sufficient to show that this model can predict the actual HGR of the battery successfully. Fig. 18 shows the training state plot of the BPNN reflecting the training model gradient = 0.043672, scalar Mu = 0.0001 and validation check = 6 at epoch 8. As can be seen in Fig. 19, the error between the actual and the target output values is mostly between −273 and 656, which is acceptable for the HGR of the battery.

Nine cases of
I
D
at 2 A, 4 A and 6 A at
T
a
of 20 °C, 26 °C and 32 °C are taken to verify the accuracy of the prediction, the predicted HGR is used to simulate battery discharge in Fluent and to compare the error of
T
f
obtained from the experiment. Results are shown in Table 4.

It is evident from Table 4 that predicted data matches well with the experimental one and their difference does not exceed 1.74 °C. These errors are within 5%, of which the maximum and minimum errors are 4.95% and 1.02%, respectively, with an average error of 3.66%. The results shows that this model can accurately predict HGR of the battery and provide an effective method for predicting and correcting HGR of batteries.

The objective of this paper is to improve the reliability of simulation studies in battery thermal management. We combine experimental and CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics) simulation to demonstrate that the predicted temperature features of battery HGR obtained from the Bernardi model differ significantly from the actual temperature behavior of the battery. This inconsistency suggests that the simulated thermal behavior using the Bernardi model is inadequate. To address this limitation, we propose a novel HGR prediction method that utilizes the BPNN method. Furthermore, we investigate the impact of
T
a
and battery
I
D
on HGR prediction. The following conclusions can be drawn.

(1)
T
a
has little effect on the low-current discharge of lithium-ion batteries, but has a greater effect on the high-current discharge of batteries. In particular, the higher
T
a
during high-current discharge, the smaller the increase of HGR can be found when the battery increase the same current discharge.
(2) In the sample of 108 groups, HGR based on Bernardi’s equation has a large error of up to 45.2% with the simulated HGR when used in CFD numerical simulations.

(3) The error between the predicted HGR based on BPNN method and the experimentally obtained HGR is less than 5%. It can be concluded that the selected BPNN model can accurately predict and correct HGR of the battery.

Chongtian Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – reviewing. Linxu Wu: Data curation, Software, Writing – original draft. Chenghui Qiu: Visualization, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jiaming Yang: Supervision, Software. Xiaolu Yuan: Validation. Yufei Cai: Formal analysis, Project administration. Hong Shi: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Research Fund of Key Laboratory of Aircraft Environment Control and Life Support, MIIT, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, China (Grant No. KLAECLS-E-202201).
